Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
Off Calendar Jul-11-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Plaintiff Hamed Bayat, in pro per present.
Atty. Daniela Bellucco, Esq. of Jackson Lewis, P.C. (tel: 619-573-4900) for the Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANTS SNAPMAGIC, INC., AND NATASHA BAKER's MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Snapmagic, Inc. and Natasha Baker's motion to compel arbitration is granted. The matter is stayed pending arbitration. The motions presently set for July 17 in this court, for sanctions and certification of litigation hold, are ordered off calendar.

Plaintiff Hamed Bayat was briefly employed at Snapmagic; Baker is its CEO. Bayat asserts wrongful termination and discrimination claims arising out of his employment and discharge. Bayat concedes that the parties' arbitration agreement covers this dispute (Opp. at p. 9:17; Bayat Dec. para. 2) but contends that defendants have waived reliance on the provision.

Contractual rights may be waived under generally applicable principles of contract law, but the court does not find here that Defendants waived their right to enforce the arbitration. "To establish waiver . . . , the party opposing enforcement of a contractual agreement must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the waiving party knew of the contractual right and intentionally relinquished or abandoned it. . . . Its intentional relinquishment or abandonment of the right may be proved by evidence of words expressing an intent to relinquish the right or of conduct that is so inconsistent with an intent to enforce the contractual right as to lead a reasonable factfinder to conclude that the party had abandoned it." (Quach v. California Commerce Club, Inc. (2024) 16 Cal.5th 562, 584.)

Here, Defendants have not answered the complaint, engaged in extensive motion practice before this court, or taken discovery. The conduct that Bayat asserts as amounting to waiver is (1) the timing of this motion, made 71 days after his demand letter, and (2) Defendants' ex parte stay application, made to avoid responding to Bayat's discovery motions. The court finds that neither supplies clear and convincing evidence of intentional abandonment of the contractual arbitration right. The former is not an unusual or significant delay, and the latter is consistent with an assertion of the arbitration agreement: Defendants moved to stay the case pending their motion to arbitrate.

Cases where courts have found waiver of the contractual arbitration provision involve longer delays and more extensive litigation conduct and are readily distinguishable. (See, e.g. Quach, supra, 16 Cal.5th at pp. 586-587 [defendant waived arbitration provision by answering the complaint, litigating for 13 months, taking plaintiff's deposition, and propounding written discovery]; Oregel v. PacPizza, LLC (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 342, 355-356 [17 months elapsed after complaint was initiated and defendant asserted arbitration agreement; defendant filed answers, demanded a jury trial, and engaged in extensive discovery]; Burton v. Cruise (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 939, 949 [plaintiff delayed arbitration demand for 11 months until "only weeks before the scheduled trial date" and after completion of discovery]; Sobremonte v. Superior Court (Bank of America Nat. Trust and Sav. Ass'n) (1998) 61 Cal.App.4th 980, 993-994 [defendant litigated for ten months, engaged in extensive motion practice including filing a cross-complaint, and engaged in extensive discovery].

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Tami Frazier, CSR# 5293 (tel: 951-233-37838 / email: tamilfrazier@gmail.com). (301/CVA)
